elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{Multi-Step TD ( SARSA($\Delta$) )} \label{alg:1} \begin{algorithmic} \State { Inputs:} Pick out the discount factors ($\gamma_0, \gamma_1, ..., \gamma_Z$), bootstrapping steps ($k_0, k_1, ..., k_Z$), and learning rates ($\alpha_0, \alpha_1, ..., \alpha_Z$). \State Set the initial value of $W_z(s, a) = 0$ for all states, actions, and scales z. \For{episode = 0, 1, 2,...}\Comment{Loop for each episode} \State Initialize state $s_0$ and choose an initial action $a_0$ in accordance with a policy. \For{t = 0, 1, 2,...} \Comment{Loop for each time step} \State Take action $a_t$, observe reward $r_t$ and next state $s_{t+1}$. \State Select action $a_{t+1}$ based on a policy. \For{z = 0, 1, ..., Z} \If{z = 0} \State $G^0 = \sum_{j=0}^{k_0 - 1} \gamma_0^j r_{t+j} + \gamma_0^{k_0} W_0(s_{t+k_0}, a_{t+k_0})$ \Else \Comment{Utilizing Eq.\ref{act-val-fun}, we substitute $Q_{\gamma_{z-1}}(s_{t+k_z}, a_{t+k_z})$ by summing the W-components up to $W_{z-1}$ in Eq.\ref{f-multi-TD-SARSA}.} \State $G^z = \sum_{j=0}^{k_z - 1} (\gamma_z^j - \gamma_{z-1}^j) r_{t+j} + (\gamma_z^{k_z} - \gamma_{z-1}^{k_z}) \sum_{z=0}^{z-1} W_{z-1}(s_{t+k_z}, a_{t+k_z}) + \gamma_z^{k_z} W_z(s_{t+k_z}, a_{t+k_z})$ \EndIf \EndFor \For {z = 0, 1, 2,..., Z} \State $W_z(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow W_z(s_t, a_t) + \alpha_z (G^z - W_z(s_t, a_t))$ \EndFor \EndFor \EndFor \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{PPO-TD($\lambda$, SARSA($\Delta$))} \label{alg:2} \begin{algorithmic} \State { Inputs:} Pick out the discount factors ($\gamma_0, \gamma_1, ..., \gamma_Z$), bootstrapping steps ($k_0, k_1, ..., k_Z$), and learning rates ($\alpha_0, \alpha_1, ..., \alpha_Z$). \State Set the initial value of $W_z(s, a) = 0$ for all states s, actions a, and scales z. \State Initialize policy $\omega$, and values $\theta^{z} \forall z$ \For{episode = 0, 1, 2,...}\Comment{Loop for each episode} \State Initialize state $s_0$ and choose an initial action $a_0$ in accordance with a policy. \For{t = 0, 1, 2,...} \Comment{Loop for each time step} \State Take action $a_t$, observe reward $r_t$ and next state $s_{t+1}$. \State Select next action $a_{t+1}$ based on a policy. \For{z = 0, 1, ..., Z} \If{$t \geq T$} \State $G^{z, \lambda_z} \leftarrow \hat{W}_z(s_{t-T}, a_{t-T}) + \sum_{k=0}^{T-1} (\lambda_z \gamma_z)^k \delta_{t-T+k}^z \forall z$ \Comment{Computing multi-step return $G^{z, \lambda_z}$ and TD-error $\delta_{t-T+k}^z$ using Eq.\ref{SRD-Estimator}.} \EndIf \EndFor \For {z = 0, 1, 2,..., Z} \State $\hat{W}_z(s_{t-T}, a_{t-T}) \leftarrow \hat{W}_z(s_{t-T}, a_{t-T}) + \alpha_z \left(G^{z, \lambda_z} - \hat{W}_z(s_{t-T}, a_{t-T})\right)$ \State $A^{\Delta} = \sum_{k=0}^{T-1} (\lambda_z \gamma_z)^k \delta_{t-T+k}^{\Delta}$ \Comment{Where $A^{\Delta}$ and $\delta_{t-T+k}^{\Delta}$ are computed using Eq.\ref{sgae}.} \State $\theta^z \leftarrow \theta^z + \alpha_z \left( G^{z, \lambda_z} - \hat{W}_z(s_{t-T}, a_{t-T}) \right) \nabla \hat{W}_z(s_{t-T}, a_{t-T})$ \Comment{Update $\theta^z$ with TD (Eq.\ref{lossfun}) using $G^{z, \lambda_z} \forall z$.} \State $\omega \leftarrow \omega + \alpha_\omega A^{\Delta} \nabla \log \pi_\omega(a_t | s_t)$ \Comment{Update the policy parameters $\omega$ with PPO (Eq.\ref{ppoloss}) for SARSA using $A^{\Delta}$} \EndFor \EndFor \EndFor \end{algorithmic}

onumber \shortintertext{We will now broaden and simplify the expressions. The immediate reward terms $r_{n+x}$ are present in both Eqs. but are adjusted by distinct discount factors, $\eta_m$ and $\eta_{m-1}$. This enables us to express the disparity in rewards as:} \sum_{x=0}^{k_m - 1} \eta_m^x r_{n+x} - \sum_{x=0}^{k_m - 1} \eta_{m-1}^x r_{n+x} = \sum_{x=0}^{k_m - 1} (\eta_m^x - \eta_{m-1}^x) r_{n+x}

elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{Multi-Step TD ( SARSA($\Delta$) )} \label{alg:1} \begin{algorithmic} \State { Inputs:} Pick out the discount factors ($\eta_0, \eta_1, ..., \eta_M$), bootstrapping steps ($k_0, k_1, ..., k_M$), and learning rates ($\alpha_0, \alpha_1, ..., \alpha_M$). \State Set the initial value of $D_m(s, a) = 0$ for all states, actions, and scales m. \For{episode = 0, 1, 2,...}\Comment{Loop for each episode} \State Initialize state $s_0$ and choose an initial action $a_0$ in accordance with a policy. \For{n = 0, 1, 2,...} \Comment{Loop for each time step} \State Take action $a_n$, observe reward $r_n$ and next state $s_{n+1}$. \State Select action $a_{n+1}$ based on a policy. \For{m = 0, 1, ..., M} \If{m = 0} \State $G^0 = \sum_{x=0}^{k_0 - 1} \eta_0^x r_{n+x} + \eta_0^{k_0} D_0(s_{n+k_0}, a_{n+k_0})$ \Else \Comment{Utilizing Eq. \ref{act-val-fun}, we substitute $Q_{\eta_{m-1}}(s_{n+k_m}, a_{n+k_m})$ by summing the D-components up to $D_{m-1}$ in Eq. \ref{f-multi-TD-SARSA}.} \State $G^m = \sum_{x=0}^{k_m - 1} (\eta_m^x - \eta_{m-1}^x) r_{n+x} + (\eta_m^{k_m} - \eta_{m-1}^{k_m}) \sum_{m=0}^{m-1} D_{m-1}(s_{n+k_m}, a_{n+k_m}) + \eta_m^{k_m} D_m(s_{n+k_m}, a_{n+k_m})$ \EndIf \EndFor \For {m = 0, 1, 2,..., M} \State $D_m(s_n, a_n) \leftarrow D_m(s_n, a_n) + \alpha_m (G^m - D_m(s_n, a_n))$ \EndFor \EndFor \EndFor \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-523: Subsequently, the delta estimators are analyzed in relation to the bias-variance trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-524: In SARSA, bias arises from reliance on current estimates, influencing updates over short time scales, especially with smaller discount factors, like lower values of $\eta$ in $D_m$.
elements.sent-2411-525: Utilizing smaller discount factors in updates leads to a decrease in variance because they depend on current estimates.
elements.sent-2411-526: However, this approach also results in heightened bias, as it does not incorporate significant information about future rewards.
elements.sent-2411-527: Conversely, variance increases for updates linked to longer time scales, as future reward information introduces additional variability.
elements.sent-2411-528: Building on this result and previous research by Kearns and Singh \cite{kearns2000bias , the analyses are adapted for SARSA to investigate the effects of bias and variance, along with the TD($\Delta$) decomposition framework, in relation to action-value functions.}

elements.sent-2411-637: By combining SARSA with TD($\Delta$) and various discount factors, SARSA($\Delta$) improves SARSA's stability and performance.
elements.sent-2411-638: This method maintains the benefits of on-policy learning while enabling a more precise breakdown of action-values.
elements.sent-2411-639: To evaluate the effectiveness of SARSA($\Delta$), experiments were performed in both deterministic and stochastic environments from OpenAI Gym, as well as in a synthetic tabular environment—specifically, the five-star ring MDP.
elements.sent-2411-640: Furthermore, enhancements in performance within dense-reward environments of the Atari suite were demonstrated through a PPO-based variant of SARSA($\Delta$).
elements.sent-2411-641: The experimental results across all examined environments indicate that the proposed method, SARSA($\Delta$), surpasses k-step SARSA TD by achieving higher average rewards, quicker convergence, and more consistent learning trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-642: The PPO-based variant of SARSA ($\Delta$) also demonstrates superior performance compared to standard PPO algorithms in dense-reward Atari environments, as evidenced by the experimental results outlined in Section \ref{secAtari .}
elements.sent-2411-643: This research contributes to our knowledge of multi-step RL algorithms and its applicability to diverse sequential decision-making tasks.
elements.sent-2411-644: Limitations: The proposed SARSA($\Delta$) method has four limitations that require attention: (i) Memory Overhead: When using a large number of $\Delta$ components, the memory needs increase since the component-level updates scale linearly with the number of discount factors.
elements.sent-2411-645: This technique requires an extra O(M) in storage compared to conventional SARSA.
elements.sent-2411-646: In certain cases, adaptive scheduling of $\eta_{m}$ can boost both the stability and the effectiveness of learning.
elements.sent-2411-647: Off-policy learning has the potential to significantly increase variance in long-horizon components, which may subsequently lead to instability during the training process.
